learning_objectives: Understand the role of QA in the software development lifecycle
Distinguish between verification and validation
Classify testing levels: unit, integration, system, acceptance
Identify the cost of defects at different lifecycle stages
readings: Myers, The Art of Software Testing, Ch. 1-2
IEEE 829 Standard for Software Test Documentation (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Software bugs cost the global economy an estimated $1.7 trillion annually in lost productivity, security breaches, and system failures. Testing is the systematic process of finding defects before users do. But testing is more than writing test cases: it encompasses strategy, design, automation, metrics, and culture. This course teaches you to think like a quality engineer. You will learn to design test strategies that balance cost and risk, write effective automated tests at every level of the testing pyramid, build CI/CD pipelines with quality gates, conduct performance and security testing, and measure quality with meaningful metrics. The goal is not just to find bugs but to build quality into the development process from the start. Prevention is cheaper than detection, and detection is cheaper than failure. By the end of this course, you will be able to design and implement a comprehensive quality assurance program for a real software project, from unit tests to production monitoring.
key_concepts: Verification vs validation: building the product right vs building the right product
Testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests, fewest E2E tests (slow, expensive)
Black-box testing: testing behavior against requirements without examining code structure
White-box testing: testing code structure by examining paths, branches, and conditions
Gray-box testing: combining black-box and white-box approaches with partial code knowledge
Test adequacy criteria: defining when testing is sufficient (coverage, mutation score, risk)
Cost of defects: exponential increase with late discovery (10-100x from coding to production)
Shift-left testing: moving testing earlier in the development lifecycle to reduce costs
Quality attributes: correctness, reliability, performance, security, usability, maintainability
Testing vs debugging: testing finds symptoms, debugging finds root causes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examine a real CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) report for an open-source project. Trace the vulnerability through the code to understand the root cause. Then write a regression test that would have caught the bug before release. Discuss what testing strategy could have prevented the vulnerability.
setup_requirements: Python 3.12+ with pytest framework installed
Git repository with a sample web application containing intentional bugs
Code coverage tool (coverage.py) configured

review.value: Testing can only show the presence of bugs, never their absence. This means testing must be combined with other quality techniques: code review catches design flaws, static analysis catches pattern violations, and formal methods prove properties for critical systems.

A bug found in production costs 10-100x more to fix than one found during coding, due to diagnosis difficulty, deployment overhead, user impact, and reputation damage. Testing shifts defect discovery left (earlier), reducing the total cost of quality.

Unit tests run in milliseconds, test one thing, and pinpoint failures precisely. E2E tests take minutes, test everything, and produce vague failures. The pyramid shape reflects the economics: invest heavily in fast, precise tests and use slow, broad tests sparingly for integration confidence.

Testing is one pillar of quality. Code review, pair programming, static analysis, architecture review, and monitoring in production all contribute. A comprehensive quality program uses all of these, weighted by project risk and team maturity.

learning_objectives: Write effective unit tests with meaningful assertions and clear failure messages
Practice test-driven development: red-green-refactor cycle
Structure test suites using arrange-act-assert and given-when-then patterns
Identify characteristics of good unit tests: fast, isolated, repeatable, self-validating
readings: Beck, Test-Driven Development: By Example, Ch. 1-8

lecture_notes.introduction: Unit tests are the foundation of software quality. A unit test exercises a single unit of code (a function, method, or class) in isolation, verifying that it produces the correct output for given inputs. Good unit tests are fast (milliseconds), isolated (no external dependencies), repeatable (same result every run), and self-validating (pass/fail with no human interpretation). Test-driven development (TDD) inverts the traditional workflow: write a failing test first, then write the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor while keeping tests green. TDD produces code that is testable by design, documented by its tests, and incrementally developed with confidence. Critics argue TDD slows initial development. Proponents counter that TDD reduces debugging time, produces better designs (because hard-to-test code is usually hard-to-maintain code), and builds a safety net that accelerates future changes. Whether you practice strict TDD or test-after, the principles of good unit testing are the same. This session covers both the mechanics and the craft: not just how to write tests, but how to write tests that are maintainable, meaningful, and trustworthy.
key_concepts: Arrange-Act-Assert pattern: set up preconditions, execute the code under test, verify the result
Test-driven development: write failing test, write minimal code to pass, refactor with green tests
Test isolation: each test runs independently with no shared state or order dependencies
Test naming: names describe behavior, not implementation (test_empty_cart_total_is_zero)
Assertion quality: assert specific values, not just 'no exception thrown'
Edge cases: empty inputs, boundary values, null/None, maximum values, unicode, negative numbers
Test fixtures: shared setup code using setUp/tearDown or pytest fixtures
Parameterized tests: testing the same logic with multiple input/output pairs
Test doubles: fakes, stubs, mocks, and spies for isolating the unit under test
FIRST principles: Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a shopping cart price calculator using TDD. Start with the simplest test (empty cart returns zero), incrementally add tests for single items, multiple items, discounts, and tax calculation. Each test drives the next piece of implementation. Show how TDD naturally produces a clean, well-structured API.
setup_requirements: Python 3.12+ with pytest
pytest-cov for coverage reporting
VS Code with Python Test Explorer extension

review.value: Red: write a test that fails because the feature does not exist. Green: write the simplest code that makes the test pass (even if ugly). Refactor: clean up the code while keeping all tests green. This cycle produces tested, clean code in small increments.

A well-named test suite is executable documentation: test_discount_applies_to_orders_over_100() describes the business rule. When requirements change, the tests show exactly which behaviors are affected. When onboarding, tests show how the system is supposed to work.

Tests that break when implementation changes (without behavior changing) are fragile and costly. Test behavior, not implementation. Assert on outputs and side effects, not on internal method calls or data structures. This makes tests resilient to refactoring.

Coverage tells you what code executed during tests, not whether it was verified correctly. 100% coverage with no assertions proves nothing. Use coverage to find untested code, then add meaningful assertions. Aim for high coverage of critical paths, not universal 100%.

learning_objectives: Design integration tests that verify component interactions
Set up test databases, mock services, and test containers
Apply integration testing strategies: big-bang, top-down, bottom-up, sandwich
Balance integration test thoroughness with execution speed
readings: Freeman & Pryce, Growing Object-Oriented Software, Ch. 8-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Unit tests verify that individual components work correctly in isolation. But software failures often occur at the boundaries between components: a function that works perfectly alone may fail when connected to a database, a message queue, or an external API. Integration tests verify these interactions. They test the contracts between components: does the service correctly query the database? Does the API client correctly parse the response? Does the message consumer correctly handle malformed messages? Integration tests are more expensive than unit tests (slower, require infrastructure, harder to debug) but catch bugs that unit tests cannot. The key challenge is balancing thoroughness with speed. Testing every possible interaction is infeasible; testing none is reckless. This session teaches systematic approaches to integration test design: identifying high-risk integration points, setting up realistic test environments using containers, and structuring tests to maximize defect detection while minimizing execution time and maintenance burden.
key_concepts: Integration points: database queries, API calls, message queues, file systems, external services
Contract testing: verifying that a service meets the interface expected by its consumers
Test containers: lightweight Docker containers providing real databases and services for tests
Test databases: isolated database instances with known state, reset between tests
Big-bang integration: test all components together at once (risky, hard to debug)
Top-down integration: test from UI down, stubbing lower layers progressively
Bottom-up integration: test from data layer up, building on verified components
API integration tests: testing HTTP endpoints with realistic request/response cycles
Transactional test isolation: wrap each test in a transaction that rolls back, ensuring clean state
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write integration tests for a REST API that manages a to-do list: (1) set up a PostgreSQL test container, (2) run database migrations, (3) test CRUD operations through HTTP endpoints, (4) verify database state after each operation, (5) ensure tests clean up after themselves. Show how testcontainers eliminates the 'works on my machine' problem.
setup_requirements: Python with pytest, requests, and testcontainers
Docker installed for test containers
Sample REST API application with database

review.value: Contract tests verify that a service provider meets the expectations of its consumers. Pact is a popular tool: the consumer defines expected interactions, the provider verifies it satisfies them. This catches integration bugs without requiring both services to run simultaneously.

Test containers spin up real databases and services in Docker for each test run. This eliminates shared test databases (source of flaky tests), ensures consistent environments (eliminates works-on-my-machine), and allows testing against the same database engine used in production.

Integration tests are 10-100x slower than unit tests. Mitigate this by: (1) running only affected integration tests on each commit, (2) parallelizing tests across containers, (3) using in-memory databases for speed-critical tests. Run the full suite on merge to main.

Most integration bugs occur at boundaries: data serialization/deserialization, encoding mismatches, timezone handling, null vs empty string, and concurrent access. Focus integration tests on these boundary conditions rather than re-testing logic already covered by unit tests.

learning_objectives: Design system tests that verify end-to-end user journeys through the full application stack
Write acceptance tests from user stories using behavior-driven development (BDD)
Use browser automation tools (Playwright, Selenium) for UI testing
Manage test data and environment configuration for system-level tests
readings: Wynne & Hellesoy, The Cucumber Book, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: System tests and acceptance tests verify the application from the user's perspective. They exercise the complete stack: UI, API, business logic, database, and external services. System tests verify that the assembled system meets its technical requirements. Acceptance tests verify that the system meets the business requirements specified by stakeholders. Behavior-driven development (BDD) bridges the gap between technical and business perspectives by expressing tests in a human-readable Given-When-Then format that stakeholders can review and validate. While system tests provide the highest confidence that the application works correctly, they are also the most expensive to write, maintain, and execute. A single E2E test through a browser can take 30 seconds and break when a CSS class changes. This session teaches you to write system and acceptance tests that maximize confidence while minimizing maintenance cost, using page object models, stable selectors, and strategic test selection.
key_concepts: System testing: verifying the complete integrated system against technical requirements
Acceptance testing: verifying the system meets business requirements from the user perspective
BDD (Behavior-Driven Development): Given-When-Then scenarios written in natural language
Browser automation: Playwright or Selenium controlling a real browser to simulate user actions
Page Object Model: encapsulating page structure in objects to isolate UI changes from test logic
Stable selectors: using data-testid attributes instead of CSS classes or XPath for reliable element selection
Test data management: factories, fixtures, and database seeding for reproducible test state
Visual regression testing: screenshot comparison to detect unintended UI changes
Smoke tests: a small subset of critical tests run after every deployment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write acceptance tests for a user registration flow using Playwright: (1) navigate to the registration page, (2) fill in the form with valid data, (3) submit and verify the success message, (4) attempt registration with an existing email and verify the error. Use the Page Object Model to encapsulate selectors. Show how data-testid attributes make tests resilient to UI redesigns.
setup_requirements: Python with pytest-playwright or JavaScript with Playwright
Sample web application running locally
Browser automation setup (Chromium, Firefox, or WebKit)

review.value: When the UI changes (a button moves, a class name changes), only the page object needs updating. All tests that use that page continue to work. Without POM, the same selector change breaks dozens of tests. POM is the single most important pattern for maintainable E2E tests.

BDD's real value is not the test framework but the conversation it enables. Writing Given-When-Then scenarios with stakeholders before coding clarifies requirements, surfaces edge cases, and creates shared understanding. The executable specifications are a bonus.

E2E tests are inherently flaky: timing-dependent (animations, network latency), environment-dependent (screen size, browser version), and data-dependent (test database state). Use explicit waits (not sleeps), idempotent test data, and retry logic with investigation of root causes.

Do not automate every user journey as an E2E test. Automate the critical happy paths (login, purchase, core workflow) and the highest-risk scenarios. Test edge cases at the unit and integration level where they are faster and more reliable.

learning_objectives: Architect a test automation framework that scales with the codebase
Implement test fixtures, factories, and data builders for consistent test data
Design a test suite structure that supports parallel execution and selective runs
Evaluate popular frameworks: pytest, JUnit, Jest, Playwright, Cypress

lecture_notes.introduction: A test automation framework is the infrastructure that supports writing, organizing, running, and reporting on automated tests. A well-designed framework makes writing new tests easy, running tests fast, and maintaining tests cheap. A poorly designed framework creates a maintenance burden that slows development instead of protecting it. This session covers the engineering of test infrastructure. We examine test fixtures that provide consistent, isolated test data without duplication. We study factory patterns that generate realistic test objects with sensible defaults. We design suite structures that support running tests in parallel, selecting tests by tag or path, and failing fast on critical errors. We evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of popular frameworks across languages: pytest's fixture system (dependency injection for tests), JUnit 5's extension model, Jest's snapshot testing, and Playwright's auto-waiting and tracing. The goal is to build test infrastructure that your team actually uses, because the best test framework is one that makes writing tests the path of least resistance.
key_concepts: Test fixtures: reusable setup/teardown code providing consistent preconditions
Fixture scoping: function (per test), class, module, session scopes for controlling lifecycle
Factory pattern: generating test objects with sensible defaults and selective overrides
Test organization: by feature, by test level, by speed, enabling selective execution
Parallel execution: running tests concurrently with isolated state (pytest-xdist, JUnit parallel)
Test selection: running subsets by tag, path, or changed-file analysis
Assertion libraries: rich assertion messages that show expected vs actual with diff
Test reporting: JUnit XML, HTML reports, CI integration for failure visibility
Snapshot testing: recording expected output and comparing against future runs (Jest, syrupy)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a test framework for a REST API: (1) create pytest fixtures for database setup/teardown, (2) implement a factory that generates user objects with random but valid data, (3) structure the suite with markers for unit/integration/e2e, (4) configure parallel execution, (5) generate an HTML report. Show how the framework reduces boilerplate in individual test files to just the test logic.
setup_requirements: Python with pytest, pytest-xdist, pytest-html, and factory_boy
Sample REST API application
CI pipeline configuration file

review.value: pytest fixtures are dependency injection for tests: declare what you need as function parameters, and pytest provides it. Fixture scoping controls lifecycle: function-scoped fixtures reset per test (isolation), session-scoped fixtures are created once (speed). This is simpler and more flexible than setUp/tearDown.

Factories generate test objects with sensible defaults: UserFactory() creates a valid user with random name, email, etc. Override specific fields for your test: UserFactory(email='duplicate@test.com'). This eliminates brittle test data setup and makes tests self-documenting about which fields matter.

Running 3000 tests sequentially takes 45 minutes; running them on 6 parallel workers takes 8 minutes. But tests must be independent: no shared database state, no shared files, no order dependencies. Fixture scoping and test containers enable safe parallelism.

Choose a framework based on: community size (documentation, plugins), CI integration, assertion quality, fixture/setup model, parallel execution support, and debugging experience. pytest dominates Python, JUnit dominates Java, Jest dominates JavaScript, and Playwright is becoming the default for browser testing.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between fakes, stubs, mocks, and spies and when to use each
Use mocking libraries to isolate units from external dependencies
Design code for testability: dependency injection and interface-based design
Recognize over-mocking as a code smell indicating poor architecture
readings: Meszaros, xUnit Test Patterns, Ch. 11 (Test Doubles)
Freeman & Pryce, Growing Object-Oriented Software, Ch. 20-21

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing a function that sends an email, charges a credit card, or queries a database presents a dilemma: you want to verify the function's logic without actually sending emails, charging cards, or requiring a live database. Test doubles solve this by replacing real dependencies with controlled substitutes. Stubs provide canned responses to calls made during a test. Mocks verify that specific interactions occurred. Fakes provide a lightweight working implementation (like an in-memory database). Spies record calls for later assertion. Each has a distinct purpose, and using the wrong one leads to brittle, misleading tests. This session covers the mechanics of test doubles and, more importantly, the design principles that make code testable. Code that is hard to test is usually hard to maintain, because the same coupling that prevents testing also prevents modification. Dependency injection, interface-based design, and the ports-and-adapters pattern all improve testability by decoupling business logic from infrastructure. The goal is not to mock everything but to design code so that mocking is rarely needed.
key_concepts: Test doubles: generic term for any substitute used in place of a real dependency during testing
Stub: returns pre-programmed responses to calls; does not verify interactions
Mock: pre-programmed with expected calls; fails the test if unexpected calls occur
Fake: a working implementation with shortcuts (in-memory database, fake SMTP server)
Spy: wraps a real object, recording calls for later assertion without changing behavior
Dependency injection: passing dependencies as parameters rather than hardcoding them
Interface-based design: depending on abstractions (interfaces/protocols) not concrete implementations
Over-mocking: excessive mocking indicates tight coupling; refactor the design instead of adding more mocks
Mockist vs classicist: testing interaction patterns vs testing state after execution
unittest.mock.patch: Python's built-in mocking for replacing objects during tests
lecture_notes.demonstration: Test a payment processing service that depends on a payment gateway API and a database: (1) use a stub for the payment gateway that returns a successful response, (2) use a fake in-memory repository instead of a real database, (3) use a spy to verify that a notification email was requested. Then refactor the service to use dependency injection, showing how it simplifies the test setup.
setup_requirements: Python with pytest and unittest.mock
Sample service with external dependencies
Dependency injection refactoring examples

review.value: Stubs answer questions: 'when the test asks for the current user, return this fake user.' Mocks verify behavior: 'assert that the email service was called exactly once with these arguments.' Use stubs when you care about the output; use mocks when you care about the interaction.

If a test requires 10 mocks to set up, the code under test has too many dependencies. This is a design problem, not a testing problem. Refactor to reduce coupling. Well-designed code typically needs 0-2 test doubles per test.

Instead of 'self.db = PostgresDatabase()', accept the dependency: 'def __init__(self, db: Database)'. In production, pass a real database. In tests, pass a fake. This pattern makes testing trivial and also makes the code more flexible and maintainable.

review.key: Don't Mock What You Don't Own
review.value: Mocking third-party APIs directly couples your tests to their implementation. Instead, create a thin adapter (wrapper) around the third-party API. Mock your adapter. If the third party changes their API, only the adapter changes, not all your tests.

learning_objectives: Measure and interpret code coverage metrics: statement, branch, path, MC/DC
Use coverage tools to identify untested code paths
Apply mutation testing to measure test suite effectiveness beyond coverage
Set meaningful coverage targets based on risk analysis, not arbitrary percentages
readings: Ammann & Offutt, Introduction to Software Testing, Ch. 2-3
Zhu, Hall, & May, 'Software Unit Test Coverage and Adequacy'

lecture_notes.introduction: Code coverage measures how much of your code executes during testing. It is the most widely used metric for test adequacy, and also the most widely misunderstood. 100% statement coverage means every line ran during tests, but it does not mean every bug was found. A line can execute without being verified: if you call calculate_total() but never assert the result, coverage counts it as covered even though the test proves nothing. Branch coverage is stronger: it requires every conditional branch (true and false) to be taken. MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage) is stronger still: each condition must independently affect the decision outcome. Even MC/DC can miss bugs that depend on specific input values. Mutation testing addresses this limitation by introducing small changes (mutations) to the code and checking whether tests detect them. A mutation that survives means the test suite has a gap. This session teaches you to use coverage as a tool for finding untested code, mutation testing for measuring test quality, and risk analysis for setting appropriate coverage targets. The goal is meaningful coverage, not maximum coverage.
key_concepts: Statement coverage: percentage of code statements executed during tests
Branch coverage: percentage of conditional branches (true/false) taken during tests
Path coverage: percentage of distinct execution paths taken (usually infeasible to achieve 100%)
MC/DC coverage: each condition independently affects the decision outcome (required for aviation software)
Mutation testing: introduce small code changes (mutations) and verify tests detect them
Mutation operators: replace operators (+/-), negate conditions, delete statements, change constants
Mutation score: percentage of mutations killed (detected) by the test suite
Coverage tools: coverage.py, Istanbul/nyc, JaCoCo, lcov, and their CI integration
Coverage gaps: code that is difficult to cover may indicate design problems (dead code, god objects)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Measure branch coverage for a date validation function. Start at 60% coverage, use the coverage report to identify untested branches (leap year logic, month boundaries), add tests to reach 95%. Then run mutation testing with mutmut to find surviving mutations (tests that do not detect a sign change from < to <=), and add assertion-focused tests to kill them.
setup_requirements: Python with coverage.py and mutmut (mutation testing)
Sample code with known coverage gaps
CI pipeline with coverage reporting configured

review.value: 100% branch coverage means every branch was executed, not that every branch was verified. Coverage measures what ran, not what was asserted. Always pair coverage measurement with assertion quality. A test that calls a function without asserting anything achieves coverage but proves nothing.

Mutation testing measures how well tests detect real changes. If changing 'x > 0' to 'x >= 0' does not fail any test, your tests have a gap at that boundary. Mutation testing is more expensive than coverage analysis but provides a much more accurate measure of test quality.

When coverage becomes a target (e.g., 'all code must have 90% coverage'), developers write low-quality tests to hit the number. Coverage should be used as a diagnostic tool to find untested code, not as a compliance metric. Use it to inform decisions, not to enforce quotas.

Set higher coverage targets for critical code (payment processing, authentication, data integrity) and lower targets for low-risk code (admin dashboards, static pages). A blanket 90% target wastes effort on low-risk code while potentially under-testing critical paths.

learning_objectives: Design and execute load tests to verify performance under expected and peak conditions
Interpret performance metrics: response time percentiles, throughput, error rate
Identify performance bottlenecks using profiling, APM tools, and database query analysis
Establish performance baselines and detect regressions in CI
readings: Molyneaux, The Art of Application Performance Testing, Ch. 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Functional correctness is necessary but not sufficient. An application that produces correct results but takes 30 seconds to respond under load is a failed application. Performance testing verifies that the system meets its non-functional requirements: response time, throughput, resource utilization, and scalability. Load testing applies expected peak traffic to verify the system handles it gracefully. Stress testing pushes beyond capacity to find the breaking point. Soak testing runs sustained load for hours to detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and gradual degradation. Each reveals different classes of defects that functional tests cannot find. Performance testing also requires understanding what to measure and how to interpret results. Average response time is misleading because it hides outliers; the 95th and 99th percentiles reveal the experience of your worst-affected users. Throughput (requests per second) shows capacity. Error rate under load shows stability. This session teaches you to design meaningful performance tests, run them with modern tools, interpret the results, and integrate performance testing into your CI pipeline for continuous regression detection.
key_concepts: Load testing: applying expected peak traffic to verify the system meets performance SLAs
Stress testing: increasing load beyond capacity to find the breaking point and failure mode
Soak testing: sustained moderate load over hours/days to detect leaks and gradual degradation
Spike testing: sudden traffic spikes to verify auto-scaling and graceful degradation
Response time percentiles: p50, p95, p99 reveal the distribution better than averages
Throughput: requests per second the system can sustain at acceptable response times
Saturation point: the load at which response time degrades non-linearly (system is at capacity)
Performance baselines: measuring current performance to detect future regressions
Profiling under load: identifying which functions/queries consume the most time under realistic traffic
k6 and Locust: modern load testing tools with scriptable scenarios
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use k6 to load test a web API: (1) write a test script that simulates realistic user behavior, (2) ramp to 500 concurrent virtual users over 5 minutes, (3) analyze the results (p95 response time, throughput, error rate), (4) identify the bottleneck using database query timing, (5) add an index to the slow query, (6) retest and verify the improvement. Show how to run k6 in CI for regression detection.
setup_requirements: k6 load testing tool installed
Sample API with monitoring and logging configured
Database with slow query log enabled
Grafana or equivalent for metrics visualization

review.value: Average response time of 200ms may hide that 5% of requests take 5 seconds. The p95 and p99 percentiles show the worst-case experience for real users. Always report percentiles in performance testing. SLAs should be defined on percentiles, not averages.

Run a standardized load test after each deployment and compare against the baseline. If p95 response time increases by more than 10%, fail the pipeline. This catches performance regressions before they reach production, just like functional test failures.

L = lambda * W: the average number of concurrent requests (L) equals the arrival rate (lambda) times the average time in system (W). If your API handles 100 req/s with 200ms average, you have 20 concurrent requests. This helps size thread pools and connection pools.

N+1 queries (loading 100 items with 101 database queries instead of 2), unbounded result sets (SELECT * with no LIMIT), synchronous external calls in the request path, and no caching of expensive computations are the most common performance bugs. Load testing reveals them.

learning_objectives: Apply OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories to test planning
Use SAST tools to find vulnerabilities in source code
Use DAST tools to find vulnerabilities in running applications
Implement fuzz testing to discover crash-inducing and security-critical inputs
readings: Takanen, Demott, & Miller, Fuzzing for Software Security Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: Security testing verifies that the system protects data confidentiality, integrity, and availability against malicious attacks. Unlike functional testing, which asks 'does the system do what it should?', security testing asks 'does the system resist what it should not do?' This adversarial mindset requires different techniques. Static Application Security Testing (SAST) analyzes source code for known vulnerability patterns: SQL injection, cross-site scripting, insecure deserialization, hardcoded secrets. Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) tests the running application by sending malicious inputs and observing responses: does the login form accept SQL injection? Does the API return stack traces to unauthenticated users? Fuzz testing generates random or semi-random inputs to find crashes, memory corruption, and unexpected behavior. Each approach finds different vulnerability types. SAST catches coding mistakes early but produces false positives. DAST finds runtime vulnerabilities but requires a running application. Fuzzing finds edge cases that no human would think to test. A comprehensive security testing strategy uses all three, integrated into the CI pipeline.
key_concepts: OWASP Top 10: the most critical web application security risks (injection, broken auth, XSS, etc.)
SAST (Static Application Security Testing): analyzing source code without executing it
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): testing the running application with malicious inputs
IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing): instrumenting the runtime to detect vulnerabilities during testing
Fuzz testing: generating random/mutated inputs to find crashes, hangs, and security flaws
SQL injection: inserting SQL commands through user input to manipulate database queries
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): tricking authenticated users into performing unintended actions
Dependency scanning: checking third-party libraries for known vulnerabilities (CVEs)
Security testing in CI: SAST on every commit, DAST on staging, dependency scan on every build
lecture_notes.demonstration: Test a deliberately vulnerable web application (OWASP Juice Shop): (1) use Bandit (Python SAST) to find hardcoded secrets and SQL injection patterns, (2) use OWASP ZAP to scan the running application for XSS and injection vulnerabilities, (3) write a custom fuzzer that generates malformed JSON payloads to crash an API endpoint. Categorize findings by OWASP Top 10 category.
setup_requirements: OWASP Juice Shop running locally via Docker
Bandit (Python SAST tool) installed
OWASP ZAP proxy for DAST scanning
Python with hypothesis library for property-based fuzzing

review.value: Integrate SAST into the CI pipeline so vulnerabilities are detected at commit time. Developers fix issues while the code is fresh. DAST and penetration testing complement SAST by finding runtime and configuration issues that static analysis cannot detect.

Most applications depend on hundreds of third-party libraries, any of which may contain known vulnerabilities. Tools like Dependabot, Snyk, and Trivy scan dependencies against CVE databases and alert on vulnerable versions. This is one of the highest-ROI security practices.

Fuzz testing has found critical vulnerabilities in every major browser, operating system, and protocol implementation. It excels at finding edge cases humans miss: buffer overflows, integer overflows, format string bugs, and deserialization exploits. Even a simple random input generator finds bugs.

Security testing finds known vulnerability patterns and random crashes. It does not find logic flaws (like a privilege escalation that requires a specific sequence of valid actions) or social engineering vulnerabilities. Penetration testing by skilled humans complements automated tools.

learning_objectives: Design a CI/CD pipeline with layered test execution and quality gates
Configure parallel test execution, test selection, and caching for fast feedback
Implement quality gates: coverage thresholds, zero failing tests, security scan pass
Set up deployment verification tests (smoke tests, canary releases)

lecture_notes.introduction: Continuous integration (CI) automatically builds and tests every code change. Continuous deployment (CD) automatically deploys changes that pass all quality gates. Together, CI/CD is the delivery backbone that turns tests into deployment decisions. Without CI, tests rot: developers skip running them, tests break silently, and the test suite becomes untrustworthy. With CI, every commit is tested, every failure is visible, and the test suite stays healthy. The design of the CI pipeline determines how quickly developers get feedback and how confidently code can be deployed. A naive pipeline that runs all tests sequentially takes 45 minutes and frustrates developers. A well-designed pipeline provides feedback in layers: unit tests in 2 minutes, integration tests in 10 minutes, E2E tests in 20 minutes. Each layer is a quality gate: if unit tests fail, do not run integration tests. This session covers pipeline architecture, test parallelization, selective test execution, caching strategies, and deployment verification. You will build a complete pipeline that balances speed with confidence.
key_concepts: CI pipeline stages: build, lint, unit test, integration test, security scan, E2E test, deploy
Quality gates: conditions that must pass before code advances to the next stage
Layered feedback: fast tests first (2 min), slower tests after (10 min), E2E last (20 min)
Test parallelization: splitting the test suite across multiple CI runners
Test selection: running only tests affected by changed files (test impact analysis)
Caching: caching dependencies, Docker images, and build artifacts between pipeline runs
Flaky test management: quarantining flaky tests, tracking flake rate, requiring fixes
Deployment verification: smoke tests after deployment, canary releases, rollback triggers
Pipeline as code: defining the entire pipeline in version-controlled YAML/code
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline: (1) run linting and unit tests in parallel as the first stage, (2) run integration tests with a PostgreSQL service container if unit tests pass, (3) run E2E tests with Playwright if integration tests pass, (4) enforce quality gates (coverage > 80%, zero failures, no critical security findings), (5) deploy to staging and run smoke tests, (6) promote to production on manual approval.
setup_requirements: GitHub repository with Actions enabled
Sample application with unit, integration, and E2E tests
Docker for service containers in CI
Deployment target (Heroku, Vercel, or AWS for demonstration)

review.value: The goal is for developers to learn about failures within 10 minutes of pushing code. Anything longer and they context-switch to other work, making the fix more expensive. Optimize for speed: parallelize tests, cache dependencies, run fast tests first.

Quality gates are automated decisions: if coverage drops below threshold, block the merge. If any security scan finds a critical vulnerability, block the deploy. These gates remove the human tendency to rationalize shipping known issues under deadline pressure.

A test that fails intermittently without code changes erodes trust in the entire suite. Teams start ignoring failures. Quarantine flaky tests immediately (mark them as known-flaky), track the flake rate, and require fixes within a sprint. Never let flakiness become normalized.

Route 5% of production traffic to the new version. Monitor error rates, latency, and business metrics. If the canary is healthy after 30 minutes, gradually increase to 100%. If not, roll back automatically. This catches production-only bugs with minimal user impact.

learning_objectives: Design regression test suites that catch bugs reintroduced by code changes
Implement test impact analysis to select relevant tests for each change
Build visual regression testing for UI components
Maintain regression suites: pruning obsolete tests, updating for requirement changes
readings: Orso & Rothermel, 'Software Testing: A Research Travelogue'

lecture_notes.introduction: Regression testing verifies that previously working functionality has not been broken by recent changes. Every bug fix, feature addition, and refactoring carries the risk of introducing new defects into existing code. The regression test suite is the safety net that catches these regressions before they reach users. As a codebase grows, the regression suite grows too, eventually taking hours to run. This creates tension between thorough testing and fast feedback. Test impact analysis addresses this by mapping code changes to affected tests, running only the relevant subset. For a change to a payment module, run the payment tests, not the entire 10,000-test suite. Visual regression testing extends this concept to UIs: capture screenshots of components, compare against baselines, and flag pixel-level differences that may indicate unintended visual changes. This session covers strategies for building, maintaining, and optimizing regression test suites that remain useful as the codebase scales.
key_concepts: Regression testing: verifying existing functionality after code changes
Test impact analysis: mapping code changes to affected tests using dependency graphs
Change-based test selection: running only tests affected by the current changeset
Visual regression testing: screenshot comparison detecting unintended UI changes
Test suite maintenance: removing obsolete tests, updating for requirement changes
Regression test prioritization: running highest-risk tests first for faster feedback
Automated bisection: git bisect + test suite to find which commit introduced a regression
Characterization tests: tests that document existing behavior of legacy code for safe refactoring
Test debt: accumulated fragile, slow, or redundant tests that slow down the pipeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up visual regression testing for a React component library: (1) capture baseline screenshots with Storybook + Chromatic, (2) make an intentional CSS change, (3) review the visual diff, (4) approve or reject. Then demonstrate git bisect with a test suite to automatically find which of 50 commits introduced a bug.
setup_requirements: React component library with Storybook
Chromatic or Percy for visual regression
Git repository with test suite for bisection demo

review.key: Every Bug Fix Gets a Regression Test
review.value: When you fix a bug, write a test that would have caught it. This test prevents the bug from ever returning. Over time, the regression suite accumulates the team's collective knowledge of past failures, becoming an increasingly valuable safety net.

If 90% of changes affect less than 10% of the codebase, running only affected tests cuts CI time by 80-90%. The investment in building a dependency graph pays off quickly in developer productivity. Tools: jest --changedSince, pytest-testmon, Bazel's test caching.

review.key: Visual Regression Catches What Unit Tests Cannot
review.value: A CSS change can make a button overlap a form field. No unit test or API test catches this. Visual regression testing compares screenshots pixel by pixel. It is especially valuable for design systems where visual consistency across components is critical.

Regression suites grow monotonically: tests are added but rarely removed. Periodically review the suite: remove tests for deleted features, merge redundant tests, and rewrite slow tests that can be covered at a lower test level. A lean suite runs faster and is easier to maintain.

learning_objectives: Apply exploratory testing techniques to discover defects that scripted tests miss
Design exploratory testing charters with clear scope, approach, and time limits
Document exploratory findings systematically for reproducibility and regression
Combine exploratory and automated testing for comprehensive coverage

lecture_notes.introduction: Automated tests verify what you expect. Exploratory testing discovers what you did not expect. It is the creative, investigative side of testing where the tester simultaneously designs tests, executes them, and learns about the system. Unlike scripted testing where test cases are defined in advance, exploratory testing adapts in real time based on what the tester observes. If a form rejects a valid email, the tester immediately investigates: what about emails with plus signs? Dots? Very long domains? Unicode characters? This adaptive approach discovers categories of defects that no predetermined test script would cover. Exploratory testing is not random clicking. It is skilled, disciplined investigation guided by charters (defined scope and objectives), heuristics (systematic variation techniques), and session management (timeboxed with documented notes). James Bach and Cem Kaner formalized these techniques into Session-Based Test Management (SBTM), which provides structure and accountability while preserving the creative freedom that makes exploratory testing effective.
key_concepts: Exploratory testing: simultaneous test design, execution, and learning
Test charters: focused missions defining what to explore, why, and for how long
Session-based management: timeboxed sessions (60-90 min) with structured debriefs
Heuristics: systematic variation techniques (boundary, CRUD, workflow, error handling)
Touring metaphors: guidebook tour, money tour, landmark tour, garbage collector's tour
Note-taking: documenting observations, questions, and bugs during exploration
Pairing: two testers exploring together for knowledge sharing and idea generation
Charter examples: 'Explore file upload with malformed files to discover handling gaps'
Transitioning findings: converting exploratory discoveries into automated regression tests
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a live 30-minute exploratory testing session on a web application: (1) define a charter targeting the search feature, (2) explore boundary conditions, special characters, empty states, and concurrent usage, (3) document 3-5 defects found, (4) write automated regression tests for the most important findings. Show the session notes format used in SBTM.
setup_requirements: Web application for exploration (can use OWASP Juice Shop or a class project)
Session note template
Screen recording tool for session documentation

review.value: Scripted tests verify that the system does what it should (confirmation). Exploratory tests discover what the system actually does (investigation). Both are necessary. Use scripted tests for regression and compliance; use exploratory tests for discovery and edge cases.

A good charter is specific enough to be actionable but broad enough to allow discovery. 'Test the login page' is too vague. 'Explore login with SQL injection attempts and unusual characters in the password field, focusing on error handling and information leakage' is actionable.

Systematic exploration heuristics: test with zero, one, many items. Test with min, max, and beyond-max values. Test CRUD operations in every order. Test interruptions (network drop, browser close, back button). Test with concurrent users. These heuristics guide exploration without scripting it.

The most valuable exploratory finding is one that becomes an automated regression test. If you discover that the search breaks on queries with a single quote, write an automated test for it. Exploratory testing feeds the automated suite with cases no one thought to script in advance.

learning_objectives: Organize test artifacts: plans, cases, runs, defects, and traceability matrices
Generate actionable test reports for different audiences: developers, managers, stakeholders
Implement test traceability from requirements through test cases to defects
Manage test environments and test data across multiple testing stages
readings: IEEE 829 Standard for Software Test Documentation
TestRail or Zephyr Documentation (test management tools)

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing produces more than pass/fail results. It produces data: which requirements are tested, which are not, which tests pass consistently, which flake, which areas have the most defects, and how quality trends over time. Test management is the discipline of organizing this data into actionable information for different audiences. Developers need detailed failure logs with stack traces and reproduction steps. Managers need trends: is quality improving? Are we ready to release? Stakeholders need confidence: does the system meet the acceptance criteria? Good test management tools and practices make this information accessible without manual effort. This session covers test plans (what, when, how, who), traceability matrices (linking requirements to test cases to defects), test environment management (ensuring consistent, reliable test infrastructure), and reporting (dashboards, trends, and release readiness assessments). The goal is to transform raw test data into decision-making tools.
key_concepts: Test plan: scope, approach, resources, schedule, risk areas, entry/exit criteria
Test case management: organizing cases by feature, priority, and automation status
Traceability matrix: mapping requirements to test cases to defects, ensuring complete coverage
Test runs and results: tracking execution status, pass rates, and defect associations
Defect lifecycle: open, assigned, in-progress, resolved, verified, closed
Test environment management: provisioning, configuration, and isolation of test environments
Test data management: creating, maintaining, and refreshing test data across environments
Release readiness report: aggregating quality metrics to support release decisions
Dashboards: real-time visibility into test execution, coverage, and defect trends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a test management workflow for a sprint: (1) create a test plan linking user stories to test cases, (2) execute test runs and record results, (3) file defects with reproduction steps and link to failing tests, (4) generate a release readiness report showing coverage, pass rate, open defect count, and risk assessment. Use a lightweight tool (GitHub Issues + labels or a spreadsheet) to avoid tool overhead.
setup_requirements: Test management template (spreadsheet or GitHub project board)
Sample project with user stories and test cases
Defect tracking system (GitHub Issues or Jira)

review.value: Traceability answers: for each requirement, what tests cover it? For each defect, which requirement is affected? For each test failure, which requirements are at risk? Without traceability, you cannot assess release readiness or prioritize test execution.

Developers need: which tests failed, stack traces, reproduction steps. QA leads need: test execution progress, flaky test trends, coverage gaps. Product managers need: which features are tested, which have open defects, release readiness. Tailor reports to the audience.

Inconsistent test environments cause 'works on my machine' failures. Use infrastructure-as-code (Docker, Terraform) to provision identical environments. Use test containers for database consistency. Use feature flags to test in production-like conditions without affecting users.

Do not buy a heavyweight test management tool unless your organization requires it. GitHub Issues with labels, a shared spreadsheet, or a simple Markdown file in the repo can manage test cases for small teams. The overhead of a tool should not exceed the overhead of the testing itself.

learning_objectives: Select and interpret quality metrics: defect density, MTTR, deployment frequency, change failure rate
Apply the DORA metrics framework for measuring software delivery performance
Build a quality culture: shared ownership, blameless postmortems, continuous improvement
Evaluate organizational testing maturity and create improvement roadmaps
readings: Whittaker, How Google Tests Software, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality is not just a technical concern; it is an organizational one. The best testing tools and frameworks are ineffective if the organization does not value quality, if developers are not incentivized to write tests, if managers prioritize features over stability, or if blame culture discourages reporting problems. This final content session examines quality from the organizational perspective. We study the DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics that correlate software delivery performance with organizational outcomes: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and time to restore service. We examine how high-performing teams build quality culture through shared code ownership, automated quality gates, blameless postmortems, and continuous improvement practices. We discuss quality metrics: which ones drive good behavior and which ones drive gaming. And we create maturity assessment frameworks for evaluating and improving an organization's testing practices. The goal is to understand that quality is a system property, not an individual responsibility, and that building quality into the process is more effective than testing quality in at the end.
key_concepts: DORA metrics: deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, time to restore service
Defect density: defects per thousand lines of code (a measure of code quality over time)
Defect escape rate: percentage of defects found by users vs found by testing (lower is better)
Mean time to recovery (MTTR): average time from failure detection to service restoration
Test effectiveness: defects found / (defects found + defects escaped)
Blameless postmortems: analyzing incidents for systemic causes, not individual fault
Quality culture: shared ownership where developers own quality, not a separate QA team
Testing maturity model: levels from ad-hoc testing to fully automated continuous delivery
Continuous improvement: retrospectives, action items, and measurable progress on quality goals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze DORA metrics from a real team's CI/CD pipeline data: calculate deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and MTTR from git logs and incident reports. Identify the team as low, medium, high, or elite performing. Create an improvement plan with specific, measurable actions to advance one level.
setup_requirements: Git log data from a sample project (or the class project)
Incident/defect tracking data
DORA metrics calculation spreadsheet
Quality maturity assessment template

review.value: The Accelerate research (2014-2023) shows that high-performing teams deploy 973x more frequently than low performers, have 6570x faster lead times, 3x lower change failure rates, and 6570x faster recovery times. Quality and speed are not trade-offs; they are correlated. Fast teams are also reliable teams.

When a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric. 100% coverage achieved with meaningless assertions is worse than 80% coverage of meaningful tests. Lines of test code per developer incentivizes verbose tests. Use metrics as diagnostic indicators, not performance targets.

After an incident, ask 'why did the system allow this to happen?' not 'who made the mistake?' Individuals make errors because the system permits them. Fix the system: add automated checks, improve monitoring, strengthen quality gates. Blame-free analysis produces better outcomes than blame-driven punishment.

Quality is not a QA team's responsibility; it is everyone's. Developers write tests, review each other's code, and own the monitoring of their services in production. QA engineers focus on test strategy, tooling, and coaching rather than manually testing developer output.

learning_objectives: Synthesize all course topics into a coherent quality assurance philosophy
Present and defend a comprehensive QA strategy for a real software project
Evaluate peer QA strategies with constructive feedback
Identify areas for continued professional development in quality engineering
readings: Course notes review: all previous sessions
Selected team QA strategy documents for peer review

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together all 14 weeks of material through capstone presentations where each team presents their comprehensive QA strategy and demonstrates their testing infrastructure. Teams have been applying course concepts to a real software project throughout the semester, building a multi-level test suite, CI/CD pipeline, and quality monitoring system. Each presentation covers the team's testing philosophy, test strategy document, automated test suite at all levels (unit, integration, E2E), CI/CD pipeline with quality gates, performance and security testing results, and quality metrics dashboard. The class evaluates each presentation against the principles learned throughout the course, providing constructive feedback. This session also serves as a comprehensive review, connecting each week's topic to the others: how TDD from Week 2 feeds the regression suite from Week 11, how security testing from Week 9 integrates into the CI pipeline from Week 10, how quality metrics from Week 14 measure the effectiveness of all other testing activities. The goal is to demonstrate not just knowledge of individual testing techniques but the ability to design and implement a cohesive quality strategy for a real project.
key_concepts: QA strategy synthesis: combining all testing levels, techniques, and processes into a cohesive plan
Risk-based test allocation: investing testing effort proportionally to risk and impact
Continuous quality: quality built into every stage of development, not bolted on at the end
Test automation ROI: measuring the value of automation investment against manual testing cost
Quality engineering career: roles, skills, certifications, and industry trends
Shift-left and shift-right: testing earlier in development and monitoring later in production
Testing in production: feature flags, canary releases, A/B testing, and observability
DevOps and quality: how CI/CD, infrastructure as code, and monitoring enable quality at speed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Team capstone presentations: each team demonstrates their complete QA implementation including automated test suites, CI/CD pipeline, quality dashboards, and a live exploratory testing session. Followed by peer review using a standardized evaluation rubric.
setup_requirements: Team capstone projects prepared and deployed
Presentation setup with live demonstration capability
Peer evaluation rubrics distributed

review.value: Testing is a system: unit tests catch logic errors, integration tests catch interface errors, E2E tests catch workflow errors, performance tests catch scalability errors, security tests catch vulnerability errors, and quality metrics measure how well it all works. Each level catches what the others miss.

Companies with strong quality practices deploy faster, recover quicker, and have higher customer satisfaction. Netflix, Google, and Amazon invest heavily in testing infrastructure because it enables rapid, reliable delivery. Quality is not a cost center; it is a competitive advantage.

Testing tools and practices evolve rapidly. AI-assisted test generation, mutation testing at scale, production testing with feature flags, and chaos engineering are active areas of development. Stay current through conferences (STAREAST, Agile Testing Days), communities, and continuous experimentation.

Good testers are curious, skeptical, and systematic. They ask 'what if?' and 'what else?' They explore boundaries, challenge assumptions, and think adversarially. This mindset is valuable not just in testing but in software design, security, and product development.

quizzes.topic: Testing Fundamentals, Unit Testing, and Integration Testing

questions.question: A function calculates shipping cost based on weight (in kg). Rules: weight <= 0 is invalid, 0 < weight <= 5 costs $5, 5 < weight <= 20 costs $10, weight > 20 costs $10 + $0.50 per kg over 20. Design test cases using equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis. List all equivalence classes and at least 8 test cases with expected results.
questions.answer:
Equivalence classes: (1) Invalid: weight <= 0 (2) Tier 1: 0 < weight <= 5 (3) Tier 2: 5 < weight <= 20 (4) Tier 3: weight > 20.

Boundary values and test cases:
1. weight = -1: invalid (error/exception)
2. weight = 0: boundary, invalid
3. weight = 0.01: just above 0, expect $5
4. weight = 3: middle of tier 1, expect $5
5. weight = 5: upper boundary of tier 1, expect $5
6. weight = 5.01: just above 5, expect $10
7. weight = 12: middle of tier 2, expect $10
8. weight = 20: upper boundary of tier 2, expect $10
9. weight = 20.01: just above 20, expect $10.005
10. weight = 50: middle of tier 3, expect $10 + $0.50*30 = $25

rubric.key: Correct equivalence classes identified (all four)
Boundary values at each class edge (0, 5, 20)
Expected results for each test case are correct

questions.question: Explain the difference between a stub and a mock with a concrete example. When would you use each?
questions.answer:
A stub provides canned answers to calls made during a test: stub_payment_gateway.charge() always returns {'status': 'success', 'id': 'fake-123'}. You use a stub when the test needs a dependency to return specific data but you do not care how or how many times it was called.

A mock is pre-programmed with expectations about how it will be called: mock_email_service.send.assert_called_once_with(to='user@example.com', subject='Welcome'). The test fails if the expected call does not happen. You use a mock when the test needs to verify that a specific interaction occurred.

Use stubs when testing return values and state changes. Use mocks when testing side effects and interactions.

rubric.key: Correct definition with concrete example for stub
Correct definition with concrete example for mock
Explains when to use each with reasoning

questions.question: Describe three strategies to make integration tests faster and more reliable while maintaining their value. For each, explain the trade-off involved.
questions.answer:
1. Use test containers instead of shared test databases. Each test run gets a fresh, isolated database. Trade-off: startup time for containers (mitigated by caching images) and requires Docker on CI.

2. Use transactional test isolation: wrap each test in a database transaction that rolls back after the test. Trade-off: does not test commit-level behavior and some operations (DDL, multi-transaction workflows) cannot be wrapped.

3. Parallel test execution with isolated database schemas or containers per worker. Trade-off: increased CI resource consumption and requires tests to be truly independent with no shared state.

rubric.key: Three distinct, viable strategies
Clear trade-off for each strategy
Demonstrates understanding of integration test challenges

quizzes.topic: System Testing, Automation Frameworks, and Mocking

questions.question: Explain the Page Object Model pattern for E2E testing. Write a Page Object class in Python (using Playwright) for a login page with email, password, and submit button. Show how a test would use this page object.
questions.answer:
class LoginPage:
    def __init__(self, page):
        self.page = page
        self.email_input = page.locator('[data-testid="email"]')
        self.password_input = page.locator('[data-testid="password"]')
        self.submit_button = page.locator('[data-testid="login-submit"]')
        self.error_message = page.locator('[data-testid="login-error"]')

    async def navigate(self):
        await self.page.goto('/login')

    async def login(self, email, password):
        await self.email_input.fill(email)
        await self.password_input.fill(password)
        await self.submit_button.click()

    async def get_error(self):
        return await self.error_message.text_content()

# Test using the page object:
async def test_login_with_invalid_credentials(page):
    login = LoginPage(page)
    await login.navigate()
    await login.login('bad@email.com', 'wrongpass')
    error = await login.get_error()
    assert error == 'Invalid email or password'

Benefits: If selectors change, only the page object is updated. Tests remain readable and focused on behavior.

rubric.key: Page object class encapsulates selectors
Uses data-testid for stable selectors
Methods represent user actions, not page internals
Test uses page object cleanly without accessing internals
Explains the benefit (isolation of UI changes)

questions.question: A CI pipeline runs 3000 tests taking 45 minutes total. Developers complain about slow feedback. Propose three strategies to reduce feedback time while maintaining test quality, ordered by implementation difficulty.
questions.answer:
1. (Easy) Layered execution: run fast unit tests first (2 min), report results immediately, then run slower integration and E2E tests. Developers get initial feedback quickly and can start fixing while the full suite runs.

2. (Medium) Parallel execution: split tests across 6 CI runners using pytest-xdist or similar. 45 min / 6 = ~8 min. Requires tests to be independent (no shared state, no order dependencies).

3. (Hard) Test impact analysis: use dependency tracking to run only tests affected by changed files. Most commits affect <10% of code, so only ~300 of 3000 tests need to run. Requires building a code-to-test dependency graph. Run full suite on merge to main as a safety net.

rubric.key: Three distinct strategies in order of difficulty
Each strategy has implementation details
Addresses quality maintenance alongside speed

questions.question: What is 'over-mocking' and why is it a code smell? Give a concrete example of a test with too many mocks and explain what refactoring would reduce the need for mocking.
questions.answer:
Over-mocking means a test requires so many mocks that it is testing interactions with mocks rather than real behavior. Example: testing OrderService.place_order() requires mocking the database, payment gateway, email service, inventory service, logging service, and metrics service. The test becomes a mirror of the implementation rather than a verification of behavior.

This is a code smell because it indicates OrderService has too many responsibilities (violates Single Responsibility Principle). Refactoring: extract a PaymentProcessor class that handles payment + email notification, an InventoryManager that handles stock checks, etc. Now OrderService coordinates these, and each can be tested with 0-1 mocks. The over-mocking was caused by tight coupling, not by the testing tool.

rubric.key: Correct definition of over-mocking
Concrete example showing excessive mocks
Identifies root cause as design/coupling problem
Proposes refactoring that reduces mock count

quizzes.topic: Code Coverage, Performance Testing, and Security Testing

questions.question: Explain the difference between statement coverage and branch coverage. Give a Python code example where 100% statement coverage does NOT achieve 100% branch coverage. Then explain what mutation testing adds beyond both.
questions.answer:
Statement coverage requires every line to execute at least once. Branch coverage requires every conditional branch (true and false) to be taken.

Example:
def check(x):
    result = 'default'
    if x > 0:
        result = 'positive'
    return result

Test: check(5) achieves 100% statement coverage (all lines execute). But branch coverage is 50%: the true branch is taken, but the fall-through (x <= 0) is not. Adding check(-1) achieves 100% branch coverage.

Mutation testing goes further: it modifies the source code (e.g., changes 'x > 0' to 'x >= 0') and checks if any test detects the change. If check(0) is not tested, the mutation 'x >= 0' survives because check(5) and check(-1) both produce the same results. Mutation testing measures whether tests actually verify the correct behavior at boundaries, not just that code executed.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of statement and branch coverage
Valid code example showing the gap
Explains what mutation testing adds beyond coverage
Concrete mutation example

questions.question: Explain what a load test, stress test, and soak test each measure. For each, give a real-world scenario where it would detect a critical defect that the other two would miss.
questions.answer:
Load test: measures system behavior under expected peak load. Scenario: verifying an e-commerce site handles 10,000 concurrent users on Black Friday without response time exceeding 500ms. Detects: capacity limits under normal peak conditions.

Stress test: finds the breaking point by increasing load beyond capacity. Scenario: determining how many concurrent video streams a media server can handle before it crashes vs degrades gracefully. Detects: failure modes (does it crash, slow down, or shed load?) that only appear beyond capacity.

Soak test: runs sustained moderate load for 12-72 hours to detect gradual degradation. Scenario: a banking API processing transactions continuously for a weekend reveals a connection pool that leaks 1 connection per hour, exhausting the pool after 48 hours. Detects: memory leaks, connection leaks, log file growth, and resource exhaustion that only manifest over time.

rubric.key: Correct definition of all three test types
Valid unique scenario for each showing what only it would detect
Distinguishes what each uniquely reveals

questions.question: Name three OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories and for each, describe a specific test you would write to detect it.
questions.answer:
1. A01 Broken Access Control: Test that a regular user cannot access admin endpoints. Send an authenticated request as user_role='viewer' to DELETE /api/admin/users/123. Assert that the response is 403 Forbidden, not 200 OK.

2. A03 Injection (SQL): Test that user input in a search field does not execute SQL. Submit the search query "'; DROP TABLE users; --" and verify that (a) the response is a normal search result or validation error, (b) the users table still exists, (c) the application logs the suspicious input.

3. A07 Identification and Authentication Failures: Test brute force protection. Send 10 rapid login attempts with wrong passwords for the same account. Verify that the account is temporarily locked or that CAPTCHA is triggered after the 5th attempt. Assert that the 11th attempt returns 429 Too Many Requests.

rubric.key: Three valid OWASP Top 10 categories identified
Specific, executable test for each category
Tests verify both positive and negative outcomes

quizzes.topic: CI/CD Pipelines, Regression Testing, and Exploratory Testing

questions.question: Design a CI/CD pipeline for a web application with 5 stages. For each stage, specify what tests run, the quality gate condition, and the approximate duration target. Explain what happens when a quality gate fails.
questions.answer:
Stage 1 - Lint & Unit Tests (target: 2 min): Run ESLint/flake8 and all unit tests in parallel. Gate: zero lint errors, all unit tests pass, coverage >= 80%. On failure: block the pipeline, notify the developer.

Stage 2 - Integration Tests (target: 8 min): Run API integration tests with test containers (PostgreSQL, Redis). Gate: all integration tests pass. On failure: block, notify developer with test failure details.

Stage 3 - Security Scan (target: 5 min): Run SAST (Bandit/Semgrep) and dependency vulnerability scan (Trivy). Gate: no critical or high vulnerabilities. On failure: block, notify developer and security team.

Stage 4 - E2E Tests (target: 15 min): Deploy to staging, run Playwright E2E tests for critical user journeys. Gate: all E2E tests pass, no visual regressions. On failure: block, notify team with screenshots.

Stage 5 - Deploy to Production (manual gate): Deploy canary (5% traffic), monitor error rate and latency for 30 min. Gate: error rate < 0.1%, p99 latency < 500ms. On failure: automatic rollback, notify on-call.

rubric.key: Five distinct stages with appropriate tests
Quality gate conditions are specific and measurable
Duration targets are realistic
Failure handling is actionable

questions.question: Write an exploratory testing charter for the search feature of an e-commerce website. Include the mission, areas to explore, techniques to apply, and how you will document findings.
questions.answer:
Charter: Explore the product search feature to discover defects in input handling, result accuracy, and performance.

Mission: Investigate how search handles edge cases, special characters, and concurrent usage. Focus on data integrity and user experience.

Areas: (1) Input handling: empty search, single character, very long queries (1000+ chars), SQL injection attempts, XSS payloads, unicode/emoji, HTML tags. (2) Result accuracy: misspellings, partial matches, synonym handling, out-of-stock items, recently added products. (3) Performance: search with 0 results, search returning 10,000 results, rapid repeated searches, search during high server load. (4) Filters and sorting: combining multiple filters, clearing filters, sorting with active filters, boundary prices.

Techniques: Boundary value analysis for input length, CRUD operations on search terms, error guessing for special characters, soap opera testing for emotional user journeys.

Documentation: Session notes with timestamps, screenshots of each bug, severity classification, and a list of bugs to convert to automated regression tests.

Timeframe: 60-minute session.

rubric.key: Clear, focused mission statement
Comprehensive areas with specific examples
Named exploration techniques
Documentation plan for findings

questions.question: Explain the difference between regression testing and retesting. Then describe how test impact analysis can reduce regression test execution time by 80% without reducing defect detection.
questions.answer:
Retesting: re-executing a specific test case that previously failed, after the defect has been fixed, to verify the fix works. It targets a specific known bug.

Regression testing: re-executing the broader test suite (or a relevant subset) to verify that the fix did not break anything else. It targets unknown side effects of changes.

Test impact analysis maps source code to tests: changing payment_service.py affects test_payment.py, test_checkout.py, and test_order.py (20 tests), not test_search.py or test_profile.py (2980 tests). By analyzing import graphs and call chains, the system determines that only 20 of 3000 tests are relevant to the change. Running only those 20 tests takes 0.3 minutes instead of 45 minutes (99.3% reduction) while catching any regression in the changed code. The full suite runs on merge to main as a safety net, catching any inter-module regressions the analysis missed.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between retesting and regression testing
Explains test impact analysis mechanism
Addresses how defect detection is maintained

quizzes.topic: Test Management, Quality Metrics, and Quality Culture

questions.question: A team has the following DORA metrics: deploys once per month, lead time of 3 weeks, change failure rate of 25%, and MTTR of 48 hours. Classify their performance level, identify the two most impactful areas for improvement, and propose specific actions for each.
questions.answer:
Performance level: Low performer (per Accelerate benchmarks: elite teams deploy multiple times per day with <1 hour lead time, <5% failure rate, and <1 hour MTTR).

Most impactful improvement area 1: Change failure rate (25%). This means 1 in 4 deployments causes an incident. Actions: (a) Add automated test quality gates to CI - block merges if coverage drops or tests fail. (b) Require code review before merge. (c) Add integration and E2E tests for critical paths. Target: reduce to <15% in 3 months.

Most impactful improvement area 2: MTTR (48 hours). This means incidents take 2 full days to resolve. Actions: (a) Implement structured logging and distributed tracing for faster diagnosis. (b) Set up alerting on error rate spikes so issues are detected immediately, not by users. (c) Create runbooks for common failure modes. (d) Practice incident response with periodic drills. Target: reduce to <4 hours in 3 months.

Improving these two metrics (lower failure rate means fewer incidents, lower MTTR means faster recovery) will also improve confidence, enabling more frequent deployments and shorter lead times.

rubric.key: Correct performance classification with benchmark reference
Two most impactful areas identified with reasoning
Specific, actionable improvement proposals for each
Measurable targets included

questions.question: Explain why Goodhart's Law is particularly dangerous for testing metrics. Give two concrete examples of metrics that drive perverse behavior when used as targets, and propose better alternatives.
questions.answer:
Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. People optimize for the metric rather than the underlying goal.

Example 1: Code coverage as a target (e.g., 'all code must have 95% coverage'). Perverse behavior: developers write tests that call functions without asserting anything, or add trivial tests for getters/setters while ignoring complex business logic. The metric hits 95% but test quality is poor. Better alternative: use mutation testing score for critical modules (measures whether tests actually detect changes) and code coverage as a diagnostic tool to find untested areas.

Example 2: Number of bugs found as a tester performance metric. Perverse behavior: testers report trivial issues (typos, cosmetic) to inflate their count, or split one bug into multiple reports. Time is spent on low-value findings instead of discovering critical defects. Better alternative: measure defect escape rate (bugs found by users / total bugs). This rewards finding important bugs before users do, regardless of count.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of Goodhart's Law for testing
Two concrete examples with perverse behaviors
Better alternative metrics proposed for each

questions.question: What is a blameless postmortem and why does it produce better outcomes than blame-driven incident response? Describe the structure of a postmortem document.
questions.answer:
A blameless postmortem analyzes an incident to understand systemic causes and prevent recurrence, without assigning blame to individuals. It produces better outcomes because: (1) People report incidents and near-misses honestly when they are not punished, giving the organization more data. (2) Focus shifts from 'who made the mistake' to 'why did the system allow this' - leading to systematic fixes (automation, guardrails, monitoring) rather than individual warnings. (3) Teams learn from failures rather than hiding them.

Postmortem document structure:
- Title and date
- Summary: what happened, when, and impact (users affected, duration, severity)
- Timeline: chronological sequence of events from detection to resolution
- Root cause analysis: systemic factors that allowed the incident (not 'Bob pushed bad code' but 'the CI pipeline did not run integration tests on this path')
